title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence

regions.name: Middle East
overview: Burgenstock nuclear talks launched 19 June: VP Vance, Witkoff, Kushner met FM Araghchi and Speaker Ghalibaf; 60-day nuclear negotiation formally underway; deal text to be published within 24-48 hours; Qatar and Pakistan attending as witnesses; Hormuz commercial traffic normalising for first time in 2026.

Lebanon escalation under MOU: Hezbollah kills 4 IDF soldiers including tank battalion commander on 19 June — deadliest Hezbollah engagement since MOU signing; Israel vows continued operations; Hezbollah has not formally accepted MOU Lebanon component and continues attacks despite Iranian MOU obligations.

Hormuz and sanctions normalising: First commercial tankers transited Hormuz under MOU protocol without incident; Lloyd's of London revised war-risk premiums downward for first time in 2026; Yanbu and Saudi Aramco export normalisation underway; $300B investment fund framework under active discussion.

Gaza and Red Sea unresolved: No Gaza political resolution in MOU; ceasefire violations surpass 3,300 since October 2025 truce; IDF targeted killing campaign continues; Houthis signal possible maintenance of maritime restrictions citing unresolved Palestinian situation; MARAD 2026-006 still in force.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 19 Jun — Burgenstock: Nuclear talks launched with no Gaza political resolution in MOU framework; Palestinian Authority formally requests post-MOU phase address Gaza's political status; PA says ceasefire consolidation is inseparable from nuclear deal.

18 Jun — Gaza Strip: UN humanitarian coordinator reports cumulative IDF ceasefire violations surpass 3,300 since October 2025 truce; food access continues to deteriorate despite Rafah partial opening.

16 Jun — Gaza City: IDF targeted killing campaign eliminates senior Hamas financial cell coordinator in airstrike on Khan Younis; Hamas military wing rearmament network disrupted but not dismantled per IDF assessment.
13 Jun — Rafah: Egypt admits 200+ aid trucks through Rafah crossing over 72-hour humanitarian window; crossing reverts to restricted status due to ongoing security concerns.
countries.coa: The October 2025 ceasefire remains nominal — violations now exceed 3,300 and the IDF targeted killing campaign is unchanged. The Iran-US MOU launched at Burgenstock on 19 June contains no Gaza-specific resolution; post-MOU diplomatic energy will be consumed by 60-day nuclear negotiations. No political resolution within 90 days; humanitarian conditions continue deteriorating; the war has no credible endpoint within the near-term horizon.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 19 Jun — Southern Lebanon: Hezbollah fires anti-tank missiles and mortars at IDF positions; 4 soldiers killed including tank battalion commander — deadliest Hezbollah engagement since MOU signing; Israel vows response within its operations framework.
18 Jun — Beirut: IDF confirms Hezbollah continues attacking troops in southern Lebanon despite US-Iran MOU; IDF strikes Hezbollah operative 'posing a threat' in south Lebanon.
16 Jun — Southern Lebanon: IDF conducts targeted strike on Hezbollah logistics position near Nabatieh; secondary explosion confirms weapons cache destroyed.
14 Jun — Beirut: Lebanese PM Salam publicly presses Hezbollah to accept MOU Lebanon component; Hezbollah remains silent on formal compliance; Litani withdrawal demand still rejected.
countries.coa: The June 19 Hezbollah attack killing 4 IDF soldiers, including a tank battalion commander, is the deadliest engagement since MOU signing and demonstrates Hezbollah's continued defiance of the deal's Lebanon component. While Burgenstock formally launched the same day, Lebanon implementation requires Hezbollah compliance that remains absent. If Iran applies effective pressure during the 60-day nuclear window, de-escalation is achievable; if Hezbollah defies Iranian instructions, the multi-front risk profile returns. Threat maintained at HIGH.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 19 Jun — Burgenstock (CH): VP Vance, Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner meet FM Araghchi and Speaker Ghalibaf at Burgenstock resort; 60-day nuclear talks formally launched; Qatar and Pakistan present as witnesses; MOU deal text to be published within 24-48 hours.

18 Jun — Tehran: FM Araghchi confirms MOU covers cessation of hostilities in Lebanon, Hormuz normalisation, phased sanctions rollback, and nuclear timeline; IRGC phase-1 Syria drawdown confirmed underway.
15 Jun — Strait of Hormuz: First commercial tankers transit Hormuz under MOU protocol without incident; Lloyd's of London revises war-risk premiums downward for first time in 2026.
13 Jun — Tehran: IRGC phase-1 drawdown from Syria underway; approximately 240 advisory personnel redeployed to Iran per MOU compliance obligations confirmed by Iranian state media.
countries.coa: The Iran-US MOU is in force and nuclear talks formally launched at Burgenstock on 19 June — the most significant de-escalation milestone of the conflict. However, the deal is at its most fragile: Hezbollah is not complying with the Lebanon component (4 IDF soldiers killed on Burgenstock day one), IRGC drawdown from Syria is in early phases, and 60-day nuclear negotiations are contentious. Reversibility risk is high. CRITICAL maintained pending successful implementation through the Burgenstock process.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 18 Jun — Sana'a: Houthi senior council signals possible maintenance of maritime restrictions citing unresolved Palestinian situation; MOU-sceptic faction reportedly gaining influence within al-Houthi's inner circle.

16 Jun — Red Sea: MARAD 2026-006 remains in force; commercial shipping diversions to Cape of Good Hope continue; industry assessment — even with immediate Houthi ceasefire, mine clearance and insurance normalisation take 90+ days.
14 Jun — Bab al-Mandab: Houthi patrol vessels conduct daylight surveillance operation in Bab al-Mandab strait; no confirmed attacks on commercial shipping but aggressive posture maintained.
12 Jun — Red Sea: Houthis continue ballistic missile and drone campaign joined since March 2026; leadership publicly threatens Red Sea oil export blockade pending MOU compliance assessment.
countries.coa: The Houthi response to the Iran-US MOU is the primary unresolved uncertainty in the regional threat picture. Internal Houthi divisions over MOU compliance are deepening. Iran's MOU obligation to stand the Houthis down is untested — and Hezbollah's continued defiance on the same day as Burgenstock suggests Iranian enforcement leverage may be limited. MARAD 2026-006 remains operative. Houthi formal response to Burgenstock is the critical near-term indicator.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 19 Jun — Jerusalem: Netanyahu condemns Hezbollah June 19 attack; vows continued operations in southern Lebanon; US urges restraint citing Burgenstock day-one talks; Netanyahu coalition faces competing pressures from far-right partners.
18 Jun — Tel Aviv: IDF deploys additional ground forces to southern Lebanon buffer zone in response to sustained Hezbollah attacks; US presses Netanyahu not to escalate ahead of Burgenstock.
16 Jun — Jerusalem: Netanyahu publicly states Israel will remain in Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza indefinitely; statement creates friction with MOU framework and US diplomatic position.

14 Jun — Gaza: IDF continues targeted killing campaign against Hamas financial and military network; new cell leaders targeted in Khan Younis area; campaign scope maintained as Netanyahu coalition imperative.
countries.coa: Israel is under maximum US pressure to allow the Iran-US MOU to take hold while its own actions — Netanyahu's public statement that Israel will remain in Lebanon/Syria/Gaza, and continued southern Lebanon operations — are straining the Burgenstock process. The 4 IDF soldiers killed by Hezbollah on 19 June will intensify domestic pressure for escalation. Key risk: Netanyahu coalition fracture between US restraint demands and far-right coalition imperatives.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 18 Jun — Baghdad: PMF leadership awaiting formal Burgenstock instructions from Tehran before modifying operational posture; Kata'ib Hezbollah pace unchanged pending outcome.
16 Jun — Salah ad-Din: ISIS attack on security checkpoint kills 2 Iraqi security force members; 3 civilians wounded.
14 Jun — Anbar Province: US-Iraq joint patrol disrupts ISIS logistics route near Al-Qa'im; weapons cache and IED materials recovered.
12 Jun — Baghdad: Kata'ib Hezbollah receives preliminary MOU compliance guidance from Tehran; formal posture review pending Burgenstock outcome.
countries.coa: PMF posture will shift materially only after formal Burgenstock-phase instructions from Tehran. If Iranian MOU implementation proceeds, direct threat to US forces in Iraq reduces within 30 days. ISIS reconstitution in Anbar and Salah ad-Din continues independently of PMF posture and is a growing secondary concern. Burgenstock nuclear talks outcome is the primary 90-day threat indicator for Iraq.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 19 Jun — Riyadh: Aramco reports first reduction in Cape-diversion logistics costs; Yanbu oil export volumes beginning to normalize as Hormuz commercial traffic resumes under MOU.
17 Jun — Gulf of Oman: First commercial tanker transits Strait of Hormuz under MOU protocol without incident; 5th Fleet confirms transit and monitors Houthi posture.
15 Jun — Riyadh: Saudi energy ministry announces accelerated investment in Yanbu and Jubail export capacity as Houthi-risk hedge during 90-day MOU transition.
13 Jun — Riyadh: Saudi-Iran bilateral rapprochement track continues; ambassadors hold third senior-level meeting since Iran war began; economic normalisation agenda expanding.
countries.coa: Saudi Arabia stands to benefit most from the Iran-US MOU among regional actors. Hormuz reopening is underway; Yanbu diversions are unwinding; and Saudi-Iran rapprochement has reached working ambassador level. Primary residual risk: Houthi maritime operations may persist through the 90-day MOU transition pending Iran's effective enforcement of Houthi compliance. Saudi Arabia's 90-day outlook is the strongest improvement of any regional actor if the MOU holds.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 18 Jun — Deir ez-Zor: ISIS attack on residential neighbourhood kills 4 civilians; 9 wounded.
16 Jun — Homs Province: Israeli airstrike on suspected IRGC weapons depot; secondary explosion chain confirms ammunition storage destroyed; Syrian Observatory for Human Rights reporting.
14 Jun — Aleppo: HTS security services dismantle 3-person IRGC advisory cell; members detained as Syria implements MOU-adjacent compliance obligations.
12 Jun — Damascus: IRGC phase-1 drawdown begins; approximately 240 advisory personnel redeployed to Iran per MOU compliance timeline.
countries.coa: IRGC phase-1 drawdown from Syria is underway under MOU obligations, which should progressively reduce Israeli strike justifications over the 90-day window. However, ISIS reconstitution in eastern Syria continues independently, and HTS-controlled northwest remains a separate instability vector. Israeli strikes on remaining IRGC positions will continue until verifiable withdrawal is confirmed. No political resolution within 90 days.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 18 Jun — New York: Egypt FM presses for Gaza political resolution at UN side event on Burgenstock day-one; calls post-MOU framework 'incomplete without Palestinian statehood pathway'.
16 Jun — Rafah: Egypt admits 200+ aid trucks through Rafah crossing over 72-hour window; crossing reverts to restricted status.
14 Jun — Cairo: Egypt-Hamas mediation track continues; Hamas political bureau under increasing internal pressure following IDF elimination of senior cell leaders.
12 Jun — N. Sinai: Security forces arrest 2 suspects linked to Sinai jihadist cell connected to Libya-based network; weapons cache seized.
countries.coa: Egypt's diplomatic position is reinforced by the MOU — Egyptian mediation was a key enabling track for the deal, and Cairo's standing as a regional stabiliser is at its strongest in years. Egypt will press for Gaza political resolution as the post-MOU priority. North Sinai counter-terrorism continues at low level. No major escalation expected within 90 days.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 18 Jun — Amman: King Abdullah formally welcomes Burgenstock opening; calls for Palestinian statehood pathway as post-MOU obligation; Jordan-US coordination meeting on implementation monitoring.
16 Jun — Eastern Border: Security forces intercept cross-border arms cache from Syria; 4 arrested.
13 Jun — Zarqa: GID counterterrorism operation arrests 5 suspects linked to ISIL-connected network.
12 Jun — Amman: Jordan-US security coordination meeting on MOU implementation monitoring and PMF posture assessment.
countries.coa: Jordan's threat environment has reduced significantly following the Iran-US MOU and Iran's prior diplomatic assurances to Amman. Primary residual risks are cross-border smuggling from Syria and domestic political pressure of the unresolved Gaza crisis. No major escalation expected within 90 days. Threat remains MODERATE.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 18 Jun — Sirte: LNA-GNU skirmish along Sirte-Misrata corridor; 3 fighters wounded; ceasefire on central front under factional pressure.
16 Jun — Mediterranean: IOM reports 260+ migrants rescued in three operations; 4 deaths; Libyan coastal departure rate unchanged.
14 Jun — Tripoli: GNU interior ministry arrests 8 suspects with confirmed militia links in security sweep.
12 Jun — Benghazi: LNA confiscates weapons cache in Kufra region; links to Sahel trafficking network confirmed.
countries.coa: Libya's dual-government stalemate continues without a political breakthrough. GNU-LNA tensions are periodically kinetic but below full-conflict threshold. Arms and migrant flows to Europe and the Sahel continue unabated. No unification pathway within 90 days.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 18 Jun — Tunis: President Saied signs additional media regulation decree; 3 journalists detained; EU foreign policy chief issues formal concern statement.
16 Jun — Kasserine: Border security forces disrupt suspected JNIM-linked transit cell operating near Algeria border; 3 arrested.
14 Jun — Sfax: Migrant departure facilitation network broken up; 6 suspects detained in maritime coordination operation.
12 Jun — Sousse: Counterterrorism unit arrests 2 suspects linked to Libya-based radical network; material evidence seized.
countries.coa: Tunisia faces a low-level jihadist threat from Libya-Algeria-linked networks and entrenched political repression under Saied with no reform trajectory. No major security escalation expected within 90 days.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Kapotnya strike — largest Moscow attack yet: Ukraine strikes Kapotnya oil refinery on 18 June — second hit in three days; 194 drones intercepted on approach; major fires at facility supplying 40% of Moscow's fuel; 16 civilians injured; Moscow airports temporarily closed; Ukraine casts strike as retaliation for attack on historic monastery.

Russian air defenses at unsustainable rate: Ukraine's sustained deep-strike campaign has left Russia's air-defense interceptor supply at an assessed 'unsustainable' consumption rate per western intelligence; Ukraine has degraded 40%+ of Russia's primary oil-refining capacity since May.

Pokrovsk front holding: ~196 combat clashes per day maintained; Russian advance west of Pokrovsk stalled since December 2025 as Ukrainian drone overmatch limits breakthrough; Russian personnel losses approaching 1.4 million since February 2022.

Russia retaliates on Kyiv: Russia conducted reciprocal strikes on Kyiv and Ukrainian energy infrastructure coinciding with Kapotnya drone attack; Crimean coastal radar network and Osa SAM system destroyed in separate Ukrainian strike on 16 June.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 18 Jun — Moscow Oblast (Russia): Ukraine launches largest drone attack on Moscow since start of full-scale invasion; Kapotnya oil refinery hit for second time in 3 days; 194 drones intercepted on approach; fires at facility supplying 40% of Moscow's fuel; 16 civilians injured; airports temporarily closed; Ukraine cites attack on historic monastery as motive.
18 Jun — Kyiv: Russia conducts reciprocal strikes on Kyiv and Ukrainian energy infrastructure following Kapotnya attack; Ukrainian air defences activated; damage reported in Kyiv Oblast.
17 Jun — Pokrovsk sector: 196 combat clashes recorded; Russian advance west of Pokrovsk stalled; Ukrainian drone overmatch continues to limit Russian exploitation of earlier Pokrovsk capture.
16 Jun — Crimea: Ukrainian Unmanned Systems Forces strike overnight — Osa surface-to-air missile system destroyed; 5 coastal radar stations hit; weapons depots and drone workshop damaged.
countries.coa: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign continues degrading Russian oil capacity, radar networks, and air-defence interceptor supply at a pace western intelligence now describes as 'unsustainable' for Russia. The Kapotnya attack demonstrates expanding Ukrainian long-range strike capability. The Pokrovsk axis is holding against grinding Russian attacks. No major Ukrainian counter-offensive expected within 90 days — attrition through deep strike combined with defensive holding remains the strategy. Western arms continuity is the critical enabler.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 18 Jun — Moscow Oblast: Kapotnya oil refinery struck in largest Ukrainian drone attack on Moscow since full-scale invasion; second hit in three days; fires at facility supplying 40% of Moscow's fuel; 16 civilians injured; major infrastructure disruption.
18 Jun — Multiple fronts: Russia retaliates with strikes on Kyiv and Ukrainian energy infrastructure following Kapotnya attack.
16 Jun — Crimea: 5 coastal radar stations and Osa SAM system destroyed overnight by Ukrainian Unmanned Systems Forces; Russian air-defence systems under increasing pressure.
15 Jun — Moscow: Kremlin warns Iran-US MOU must not prejudice Russian regional interests; signals concern about US strategic reach in Iran post-deal; Russia-Iran axis under strain.
countries.coa: Russia's attritional strategy continues at mounting cost — approaching 1.4 million cumulative personnel losses, sustained economic damage from the deep-strike campaign, and an air-defence interceptor supply now assessed at 'unsustainable' consumption rates. The Iran-US MOU complicates Russian regional positioning. No major Russian operational breakthrough expected in the 90-day window.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 18 Jun — Gomel Oblast: Russian military logistics convoys transiting Belarusian rail corridor at increased volume ahead of Russian summer operational cycle.
16 Jun — Poland-Belarus border: Hybrid pressure continues; 320+ crossing attempts in 72-hour period; Frontex elevated status maintained.
14 Jun — Minsk: Belarus-Russia joint military exercise announced for August; scope expanding per Shoigu-Lukashenko meeting agenda.
countries.coa: Belarus remains a primary Russian logistics enabler for Ukraine operations. Lukashenko will not commit Belarusian forces directly to the war. Hybrid pressure on Poland and Baltic borders continues as a low-cost destabilisation tool.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 18 Jun — Chisinau: EU accession second formal screening chapter launched in Brussels; Moldova formally on accession timeline.
16 Jun — Transnistria: Unusual Russian logistics movements near Tiraspol continue; Moldova maintains request for increased EU monitoring.
14 Jun — Chisinau: Energy contingency plan active; Romania and Greece supply routes fully operational; no Russian gas dependency remaining.
countries.coa: Moldova faces moderate risk from Russian hybrid pressure and Transnistria destabilisation. EU accession track provides political insulation and strategic clarity. No kinetic escalation expected within 90 days.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 18 Jun — Tbilisi: EU formally suspends accession status talks following passage of constitutional civil society amendments; Georgian Dream accelerates alignment with Moscow.
17 Jun — S. Ossetia: Russian military patrol observed within 100m of administrative boundary line for second time in one week; Georgia files formal diplomatic protest.
15 Jun — Tbilisi: 14 pro-EU opposition activists detained during demonstrations; EU and US issue joint call for immediate release.
13 Jun — Tbilisi: Georgian Parliament passes civil society foreign funding restrictions; EU foreign policy chief calls measures 'incompatible with EU membership'.
countries.coa: Georgia faces dual threats: Russian military presence in occupied territories and an accelerating internal political crisis as Georgian Dream consolidates Moscow alignment. EU and NATO integration pathways are at serious risk. Civil resistance movement remains active but systematically suppressed.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 18 Jun — Yerevan: Armenia-EU Association Council holds second session; EU pledges additional €180M in security sector reform support.
16 Jun — Syunik Province: Minor firing incident on Armenia-Azerbaijan LoC; EUMA monitors confirm; no casualties.
14 Jun — Yerevan: PM Pashinyan signals parliamentary vote on formal CSTO obligations dissolution expected Q3 2026.
countries.coa: Armenia is pivoting firmly toward the EU and West following the 2023 Nagorno-Karabakh defeat. Russia-Armenia relations continue deteriorating. Risk of renewed Azerbaijani military action is moderate. CSTO formal withdrawal likely within 12 months.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 18 Jun — Baku: Aliyev reiterates Zangezur corridor demands as condition for broader regional normalisation; conditional support for MOU regional framework.
16 Jun — AZ-Armenia Border: Bilateral border demarcation working group meets in Tavush sector; modest progress reported.
13 Jun — Baku: Azerbaijani officials express cautious optimism about Iran de-escalation under MOU; AZ-Iran border remains calm.
countries.coa: Azerbaijan continues leveraging its strategic position confidently. Zangezur corridor access remains the primary diplomatic focus. AZ-Iran relations may normalise further if the MOU holds. No major military action expected within 90 days.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: Sudan El Obeid critical: 29 Western countries formally warn UN Human Rights Council of imminent RSF assault and risk of large-scale atrocities affecting 500,000 civilians (19 Jun); RSF drone campaign has killed 50+ civilians since 10 June; multi-axis encirclement of El Obeid complete; SAF counter-deployment assessed insufficient.

Nigeria counter-strike: Nigeria-US joint intelligence-led airstrike kills 21 ISWAP fighters including 3 mid-level commanders in Borno's Kukawa LGA — largest single counter-operation of June; ISWAP retains mass casualty capability following June 9 (Gubio, 81 killed) and June 13 (Monguno/Nganzai, 60+ killed) attacks.

JNIM Dosso expansion: JNIM's first confirmed assault in Niger's Dosso Region (Kirtachi, 14 June) marks southward expansion toward coastal West Africa; combined with Burkina Faso Dori siege and Mali supply stranglehold, JNIM now operates across a continuous Sahel-to-coastal arc.
Burkina Faso Dori siege: JNIM siege of Dori operational; hospital medical supplies at critical level; MSF withdrawn from 5th district; information blackouts and regime fragility indicators at peak.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 18 Jun — Mopti Region: JNIM convoy attack on FAMa resupply patrol near Tenenkou; 4 soldiers killed, 2 vehicles destroyed.
16 Jun — Bamako: Fuel rationing extended to 6 additional districts; food shortages critical in 3 regions as JNIM supply-line stranglehold tightens around Bamako.
14 Jun — Kidal: JNIM and FLA consolidate Kidal region; no FAMa counter-presence within 80km; junta unable to project force into northeast.
12 Jun — Gao: JNIM armed drone strikes FAMa supply depot for second time in 10 days; storage facility destroyed; drone proliferation rate accelerating.
countries.coa: JNIM's Bamako encirclement strategy is tightening — fuel and food rationing in additional districts signals an advancing siege dynamic. JNIM drone capability is proliferating rapidly (approximately 80 drone strikes recorded in 2025-2026 vs. under 10 in 2024). FAMa cannot dislodge JNIM without Wagner reinforcement, which is spread thin. Junta legitimacy declining. Bamako encirclement risk within 90 days remains HIGH if the current trajectory holds.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 18 Jun — Tillabéri: ISSP attack on civilian-military convoy; 7 killed, 14 injured.
16 Jun — Dosso Region: JNIM patrol active near Kirtachi following June 14 National Guard camp attack; CNSP deploys reinforcements from Niamey.
14 Jun — Kirtachi (Dosso Region): JNIM overruns National Guard camp; 12 soldiers killed, 7 taken prisoner — JNIM's first confirmed assault in Dosso Region, marking significant southward expansion.
12 Jun — Diffa: Cross-border ISWAP incursion from Lake Chad zone; 2 villages raided, 400+ displaced.
countries.coa: JNIM's Kirtachi attack marks a significant geographical expansion into Dosso Region — the closest JNIM advance to Nigeria and coastal West Africa. Combined with ISSP cross-border operations in Tillabéri and Diffa, Niger faces a multi-axis threat with no effective Western counter-terrorism partnership after CNSP expelled US and French forces. Oil pipeline sabotage by ISSP is an additional infrastructure threat vector.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 18 Jun — Dori (Sahel Region): JNIM siege of Dori enters second day; hospital reports medical supply critical; last civilian supply convoy turned back; no resupply route available.
16 Jun — Ouagadougou: 12-hour internet blackout imposed; 4 additional journalists detained in information suppression operation; civil society groups report 7 activists missing.
14 Jun — Est Region: MSF formally withdraws last staff from 5th district following direct JNIM threats; 420,000 people in affected areas without MSF health services.
12 Jun — Boucle du Mouhoun: Two villages attacked by JNIM fighters; 2,100+ displaced; IDP camps critically overcrowded.
countries.coa: Burkina Faso's trajectory remains the most severe in the region. JNIM's Dori siege is operationally complete — supply denial achieved and humanitarian situation critical. Information blackouts signal growing regime fragility. Junta survival depends on Russian-Wagner support already committed to Mali. No pathway to stabilisation within 90 days.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 19 Jun — El Obeid: 29 Western countries formally warn UN Human Rights Council of imminent RSF assault on El Obeid; 500,000 civilians assessed at risk of large-scale atrocities; UN rights chief calls for immediate cessation of hostilities.

17 Jun — North Kordofan: RSF drone campaign kills 10+ civilians on ninth consecutive day; 50+ total deaths in drone campaign since June 10; fuel stations and civilian infrastructure systematically targeted.

15 Jun — North Kordofan: RSF completes multi-axis encirclement of El Obeid — forces confirmed at En Nahud, Al-Khuwei, Bara, Jurayjikh, Kazgail and Al-Rayash; SAF counter-deployment assessed insufficient to prevent assault.
13 Jun — El Fasher (North Darfur): RSF maintains total blockade; 180,000+ civilians trapped; MSF surgical unit exhausted of supplies.
countries.coa: Sudan enters the most critical inflection point since the war began. El Obeid's fall would give RSF the strategic Kordofan-Darfur corridor and cut SAF logistics to the west — a decisive strategic shift. 29 countries have formally warned of imminent atrocities against 500,000 civilians. No ceasefire mechanism is in place. The El Obeid outcome will define the war's trajectory for the remainder of 2026.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: 18 Jun — Lake Chad (Lac Province): ISWAP attack on Baga Sola military patrol; 3 soldiers killed.
16 Jun — Tibesti Region: ANT operation against armed group with confirmed Libya connections; 2 soldiers wounded, 6 fighters killed.
13 Jun — N'Djamena: Transitional council holds extraordinary session; election roadmap postponed again without a new timeline offered.
countries.coa: Chad maintains relative stability as a regional anchor. ISWAP Lake Chad operations will not significantly escalate without further Borno-side enablement. Mahamat Déby continues delaying elections — no viable democratic transition within 90 days.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: 18 Jun — Hodh Ech Chargui: Security forces intercept JNIM-linked arms cache near Néma; 4 arrested.
16 Jun — Néma: Precautionary evacuation of border villages following JNIM's first Dosso Region (Niger) assault; spillover threat assessment elevated.
13 Jun — Nouakchott: Emergency security council following JNIM Dosso expansion; additional air assets deployed to eastern border surveillance.
countries.coa: Mauritania remains the most stable Sahel state. Primary risk is Mali and Niger spillover — JNIM's first Dosso Region attack has alarmed Mauritanian security planners. JNIM drone proliferation adds a new threat vector. No kinetic escalation expected within 90 days but security investment is accelerating.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 18 Jun — Borno (Kukawa LGA): Nigeria-US joint intelligence-led airstrike kills 21 ISWAP fighters including 3 mid-level commanders; operation targets ISWAP camp and Lake Chad movement corridors used to launch attacks.
17 Jun — Borno (Monguno area): ISWAP attack on military checkpoint near Monguno; 3 soldiers killed.
16 Jun — Zamfara: Northwest bandit coalition attacks village near Gusau; 9 civilians killed, livestock seized; security forces arrive hours later.
13 Jun — Borno (Monguno/Nganzai): ISWAP kills 60+ civilians in dual coordinated village raids — upgraded ISWAP command coordination confirmed in simultaneous multi-target assault.
countries.coa: Nigeria remains at CRITICAL following the worst ISWAP week of 2026 — June 9 Gubio (81 killed) and June 13 Monguno/Nganzai (60+ killed). The Nigeria-US joint airstrike on June 18 killing 21 fighters including 3 commanders is a significant counter-operation but has not degraded ISWAP's mass casualty capacity. ISWAP dual-target coordination is an upgraded capability. Northwest banditry and southeast separatist pressure remain unresolved secondary threats.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: China grey-zone expansion: PRC sanctions Philippines Defence Secretary Teodoro (11 Jun) — first sanctioning of a sitting minister of a PRC-recognised state; China launches maritime law enforcement patrol east of Taiwan in retaliation for Japan-Philippines boundary talks (6 Jun); CCG Kinmen/Pratas/Yellow Sea incursion rates all accelerating simultaneously.

Taiwan pressure: 132 PLA aircraft detections and 109 naval vessel detections in Taiwan AOR so far in June; Taiwan's opposition-driven budget crisis delays defence investment; first CCG-research vessel coordinated Pratas patrol marks tactical step-up.

Myanmar Kyaukphyu decisive phase: Arakan Army now controls 89% of Rakhine townships; AA Commander-in-Chief states decisive battles for remaining territories (Kyaukphyu, Sittwe) will conclude before end of 2026 or 2027; China's $1.3B deep-sea port and SEZ at Kyaukphyu directly threatened.

India-Pakistan: Ceasefire technically intact at 13 months; LoC firing continues; TTP attacks in North Waziristan and BLA infrastructure attacks in Balochistan continue; India-China LAC tensions a persistent secondary concern.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 15 Jun — DPRK: Kim Jong Un oversees 8th significant weapons test of 2026 — modular missile launcher and multiple tactical cruise missile systems designated for deployment near South Korean border.
12 Jun — Pyongyang: DPRK state media report expanded China-DPRK economic and military cooperation packages following Xi's June 8-9 state visit; Xi implicitly legitimised DPRK's nuclear demands.
14 Jun — DMZ: Satellite imagery confirms continued fortification works and military road construction along southern boundary; new trench systems under construction.
11 Jun — East Sea: DPRK naval exercise observed east of Korean Peninsula; South Korea scrambles aircraft and naval assets.
countries.coa: North Korea's 8th weapons test of 2026 demonstrates continued development momentum following Xi's implicit nuclear legitimisation during the June 8-9 state visit. DPRK is converting the China-DPRK strategic partnership into both economic leverage and military capability advancement across multiple delivery platforms. Denuclearisation probability in 90 days: zero. Additional missile and weapons tests expected.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 17 Jun — Rakhine State: Arakan Army continues advance toward Kyaukphyu port; junta airstrike kills 9 civilians attempting to flee combat zone.
15 Jun — Shan State: SCEF resistance alliance captures territory near Lashio; junta responds with air and artillery strikes causing civilian casualties.
13 Jun — Rakhine State: Arakan Army completes tactical encirclement of Kyaukphyu port garrison; China's $1.3B deep-sea port and SEZ at Kyaukphyu directly threatened.
11 Jun — Sagaing: PDF ambush on junta logistics convoy; 8 soldiers killed, vehicle destroyed.
countries.coa: The Arakan Army now controls 89% of Rakhine townships and the Kyaukphyu tactical encirclement is complete. AA Commander-in-Chief Twan Mrat Naing stated decisive battles for remaining territories will conclude before end of 2026 or 2027. China's primary Myanmar infrastructure investment — the $1.3B Kyaukphyu deep-sea port and SEZ — is directly threatened, creating pressure on Beijing to engage the AA diplomatically. Junta relies on air power to compensate for manpower deficits. No political resolution within 90 days.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 17 Jun — KPK (North Waziristan): TTP attack on military checkpost; 4 security forces killed, 2 wounded.
15 Jun — Balochistan: BLA attack on pipeline monitoring station; 2 engineers killed.
13 Jun — Peshawar: Security services arrest 7 TTP facilitators in coordinated raids; cross-border coordination network disrupted.
11 Jun — LoC (Kashmir): Cross-border firing continues; India-Pakistan ceasefire technically intact at 13 months.
countries.coa: Pakistan faces simultaneous TTP pressure in KPK and BLA infrastructure attacks in Balochistan. India-Pakistan ceasefire holds at 13 months but remains structurally fragile. Economic deterioration compounds security challenges. TTP's North Waziristan attacks signal continued pressure in the tribal belt.

countries.name: India
incidents: 17 Jun — Kashmir (Shopian): Security forces kill 3 militants in gunfight; 1 CRPF trooper wounded.
15 Jun — Arunachal Pradesh: PLA patrol crosses LAC at Asaphila sector; India-China standoff resolved within hours; India files formal diplomatic protest.
13 Jun — Manipur: Ethnic violence continues; 3 killed in Churachandpur district; security forces maintain curfew.
countries.coa: India maintains elevated military posture vis-à-vis both Pakistan and China. Manipur ethnic conflict drains domestic security resources. LAC tensions are a persistent secondary concern. India-Pakistan ceasefire remains structurally fragile despite 13-month duration.

countries.name: China
incidents: 18 Jun — Yellow Sea: Second CCG incursion into South Korean-claimed waters within one week; Seoul summons Chinese ambassador for second time in a week.

15 Jun — Pratas/Dongsha: First coordinated CCG and PRC research vessel patrol around Pratas restricted waters — CCG conducting operational patrol while research vessel surveys restricted zone perimeter.

11 Jun — South China Sea: PRC formally sanctions Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro for 'damaging PRC-Philippine relations' — first sanctioning of a sitting minister of a PRC-recognised state.
6 Jun — East of Taiwan: China launches special maritime law enforcement patrol east of Taiwan in retaliation for Japan-Philippines maritime boundary delimitation talks.
countries.coa: China is simultaneously expanding its grey-zone campaign across multiple theatres: CCG Kinmen/Pratas incursions accelerating; first CCG-research vessel coordinated Pratas patrol is a tactical step-up; Yellow Sea 'creeping sovereignty' produced two incidents in one week; sanctioning of the Philippine Defence Secretary marks a new diplomatic coercion tool. No major military action vs. Taiwan expected within 90 days but grey-zone intensity will continue to increase.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 17 Jun — Taiwan ADIZ: PLA incursion by 16 aircraft into Taiwan ADIZ; fighters and naval assets scrambled.
15 Jun — Pratas/Dongsha: CCG and PRC research vessel conduct first coordinated patrol around Pratas restricted waters; Taiwan coast guard responds with formal warning.
13 Jun — Kinmen Islands: CCG 9th intrusion into Kinmen restricted waters in 2026; Taiwan coast guard issues formal warning.
11 Jun — Taipei: Taiwan defence ministry warns of accelerating CCG Kinmen and Pratas incursion rates; budget delays from opposition parties threaten defence investment timeline.
countries.coa: Taiwan faces sustained and intensifying grey-zone pressure on multiple axes simultaneously. 132 PLA aircraft detections and 109 naval vessel detections recorded in June to date. The first CCG-research vessel coordinated Pratas patrol represents a tactical step-up. Taiwan's budget crisis, driven by opposition delays, adds a structural vulnerability. Cross-strait kinetic conflict risk is LOW in the 90-day window but grey-zone intensity will continue increasing through 2026.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: 17 Jun — South China Sea (Second Thomas Shoal): Philippines conducts resupply mission to BRP Sierra Madre; CCG shadow operation deployed in response.
15 Jun — Scarborough Shoal: CCG water cannon deployed against Philippines coast guard vessel — fourth Scarborough confrontation of June.
11 Jun — Manila: PRC formally sanctions Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro for 'damaging PRC-Philippine relations' — first sanctioning of a sitting minister of a PRC-recognised state.
countries.coa: Philippines faces persistent and escalating Chinese grey-zone pressure. The sanctioning of Defence Secretary Teodoro marks a new diplomatic escalation phase. US-Philippines alliance and France SOFA strengthen Manila's deterrent posture. Scarborough Shoal miscalculation risk remains elevated. Mindanao internal security is manageable.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 18 Jun — Seoul: ROK ministry reports second CCG incursion into South Korean-claimed Yellow Sea waters within 7 days; Chinese ambassador summoned for second time in a week.
15 Jun — Seoul: ROK NSC convenes over DPRK 8th weapons test; accelerates deployment of THAAD enhancement package.
13 Jun — DMZ: Unusual DPRK movement near DMZ; ROK military on elevated alert following North Korean naval exercise.
countries.coa: South Korea faces an expanding threat matrix — DPRK cruise missile development for South Korean border deployment and China's Yellow Sea grey-zone expansion both intensified this week. ROK will intensify US-ROK combined deterrence and accelerate trilateral coordination with the US and Japan.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: 16 Jun — Paracel Islands: Vietnam lodges second diplomatic protest in a week over CCG water cannon use on Vietnamese fishing vessels.
14 Jun — Hanoi: Vietnam-US defence cooperation MOU updated; maritime domain awareness agreement signed.
12 Jun — South China Sea: Vietnamese coast guard confronts Chinese fishing fleet inside EEZ; vessels withdraw after formal warning.
countries.coa: Vietnam faces persistent grey-zone friction with China. 'Bamboo diplomacy' maintains relations with all major powers while US-Vietnam defence cooperation quietly deepens. No kinetic escalation expected within 90 days.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 17 Jun — Tokyo: Japan Defence Ministry formally calls for emergency UN Security Council session following DPRK 8th weapons test.
15 Jun — East China Sea: MSDF destroyer deploys to monitor PLA naval transit; China launches maritime patrol east of Taiwan in retaliation for Japan-Philippines boundary talks.
13 Jun — Tokyo: Diet approves initial funding tranche for accelerated counter-strike capability programme.
countries.coa: Japan faces a deteriorating strategic environment — DPRK cruise missile development, China's retaliatory maritime patrol for Japan-Philippines boundary talks, and growing PLA activity — but no imminent kinetic threat. Counter-strike capability programme accelerating. Japan-US alliance remains the primary deterrent.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 15 Jun — Natuna Sea: Indonesian coast guard confronts Chinese fishing fleet inside EEZ; 12 vessels warned off.
13 Jun — Papua: OPM separatists attack highway maintenance crew; 1 worker killed.
countries.coa: Indonesia maintaining non-aligned posture while quietly strengthening maritime security. Natuna Sea incursions are a persistent Chinese grey-zone tactic. Papua OPM remains a low-level internal concern.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: 16 Jun — Deep South: Suspected separatist IED damages army patrol vehicle; 1 soldier lightly injured.
14 Jun — Bangkok: MARA Patani peace dialogue fourth round concluded; modest progress on cultural recognition framework.
countries.coa: Thailand's Deep South separatist conflict remains a contained low-level challenge. MARA Patani dialogue showing limited progress. Bangkok's political stability has improved.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: 16 Jun — Dhaka: Interim government extends security operations against JMB networks; 8 suspects arrested.
14 Jun — Cox's Bazar: Rohingya camp inter-gang violence injures 12; UN access restricted for 24 hours.
countries.coa: Bangladesh faces internal governance challenges post-2024 transition. Rohingya crisis in Cox's Bazar remains a persistent instability source. External threat level is LOW.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 15 Jun — Colombo: IMF fifth review underway; government meets fiscal targets; fourth tranche disbursement approved.
13 Jun — Northern Province: Tamil political delegation meets with Sri Lankan President over land restitution; constructive dialogue reported.
countries.coa: Sri Lanka is in economic recovery mode post-2022 crisis. Security environment is stable — no active conflict. Chinese debt diplomacy remains a structural strategic competition concern.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Colombia runoff 21 June: De la Espriella (far-right, 43.7%) vs. Cepeda (left-wing, 40.9%) two days out; ELN ceasefire 20-23 June to guarantee voting; result determines peace process trajectory, US-Colombia security cooperation, and military posture toward ELN, EMC and EMBF.

Venezuela post-intervention: US-Venezuela joint killing of Niño Guerrero (June 12) followed by 12 additional Maduro loyalist network arrests; oil infrastructure restoration continues — Zulia refinery at ~60% pre-intervention capacity; transitional authority recognition growing (47 states).

Cuba maximum pressure: Iran-US MOU removes Iran as Cuba's last major diplomatic ally; USS Nimitz deployment and Cupet sanctions tightening; protest movement now spans eastern Cuba; government suppressing with rapid-response units.

Haiti GSF crisis: Viv Ansanm multi-checkpoint assault on 17 June kills 2 GSF personnel; GSF at ~1,400 of 5,500-mandated; gang territorial control expanding in Artibonite agricultural zone; Chad replacement force still in US training.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 18 Jun — Caracas: 12 additional Maduro loyalist network members arrested in coordinated transitional authority security operation; weapons cache seized.
16 Jun — Maracaibo: Oil infrastructure restoration continues; Zulia refinery assessed at approximately 60% of pre-intervention capacity.
14 Jun — Colombia Border (Apure): Joint Colombia-Venezuela security operation continues; 4 armed group members killed in Apure zone clearing.

12 Jun — Bolívar State: US-Venezuela joint airstrike kills Tren de Aragua leader Hector Rusthenford Guerrero ('Niño Guerrero') — largest single blow to the hemisphere's most feared transnational criminal organisation.
countries.coa: Venezuela's post-intervention stabilisation continues. The US-Venezuela joint killing of Niño Guerrero (June 12) is the most significant organised crime dismantlement milestone and demonstrates growing operational trust between Caracas and Washington. Maduro loyalist networks will maintain asymmetric resistance targeting oil infrastructure. International recognition is growing (47 states) but not consolidated. Transitional authority lacks full territorial control in rural and border zones.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 17 Jun — Port-au-Prince: Gang coalition Viv Ansanm launches coordinated assault on three GSF checkpoints simultaneously; 2 GSF personnel killed, one position temporarily overrun before recovery.
15 Jun — Artibonite: Gang expansion into Artibonite agricultural zone continues; 2,000+ farmers displaced; food supply disruption worsening across northern Haiti.
13 Jun — Cité Soleil: HNP-GSF joint operation frees 11 kidnap victims; 3 gang members arrested.
11 Jun — Port-au-Prince: GSF strength confirmed at ~1,400 of 5,500-mandated; Chad replacement force still in US training; deployment timeline remains uncertain.
countries.coa: GSF remains critically understaffed and unable to halt gang territorial expansion. Viv Ansanm's multi-checkpoint assault on June 17 demonstrates improved gang tactical coordination. Gang territorial control continues expanding in the Artibonite — additional urban centres falling within 90 days is a HIGH-probability outcome. Chad replacement force deployment timeline must accelerate or Haiti remains ungovernable.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 18 Jun — Arauca: ELN fires on military patrol ahead of formal ceasefire start; 1 soldier wounded — ELN attacks continue until ceasefire takes effect 20 June.
17 Jun — Cauca: EMC attack kills 3 civilians and 1 police officer; military responds with airstrike on EMC base — 4 days before presidential runoff.
15 Jun — Nariño: EMBF ambush on highway police patrol; 2 officers killed.
14 Jun — Bogotá: ELN formally announces ceasefire 20-23 June — tactical pause to guarantee runoff voting; full operations expected to resume post-election.
countries.coa: Colombia's June 21 presidential runoff is the defining event of the next 90 days. De la Espriella (far-right, 43.7%) vs. Cepeda (left-wing, 40.9%) — outcome determines the peace process approach, US-Colombia security cooperation trajectory, and military posture toward armed groups. ELN ceasefire is a confirmed tactical pause; full operations resume post-election. EMC and EMBF operations continue regardless of electoral outcome.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 17 Jun — Havana: Iran-US MOU removes Iran as Cuba's primary diplomatic ally; FM Rodriguez condemns MOU as 'abandonment of oppressed peoples'; Cuba is now near-totally isolated diplomatically.
15 Jun — Santiago de Cuba: Power outages trigger demonstrations across eastern Cuba for fourth consecutive week; rapid-response units deployed; 6 arrested.
13 Jun — Havana University: Pro-democracy sit-in; 6 arrested; international academic bodies condemn detentions.
11 Jun — New York: Cuba requests UN Security Council emergency session on USS Nimitz deployment; US veto applied.
countries.coa: Cuba's energy and humanitarian crisis is deepening rapidly. The Iran-US MOU removes Cuba's last major diplomatic ally, leaving it near-totally isolated. US sanctions on Cupet and USS Nimitz deployment maintain maximum pressure. The protest movement is expanding geographically to eastern Cuba. Government will suppress militarily but faces growing capacity constraints. Risk of regime-threatening upheaval within 6-12 months is elevated.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 17 Jun — Esmeraldas: Multi-cartel conflict kills 4 including 2 police officers; state of exception extended for fourth consecutive period.
15 Jun — Guayaquil: Los Lobos faction retaliatory attack on R7-linked warehouse; 5 killed.
13 Jun — Quito: Government security council reports 340 homicides in May — 15% above 2025 monthly average.
countries.coa: Ecuador faces entrenched cartel violence (Los Lobos, R7, La Firma) that has outpaced state capacity. State of exception provides temporary relief but no structural improvement expected within 90 days.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 17 Jun — VRAEM: SL-VRAEM attack on police eradication team; 2 officers killed.
15 Jun — Lima: Anti-government protests over inflation; 8,000 demonstrators; peaceful.
13 Jun — Loreto: 4 killed in illegal mining territorial dispute near Colombian border.
countries.coa: Peru's challenges remain primarily internal: SL-VRAEM insurgency, illegal mining violence, and political instability. President Boluarte government is fragile but functional. No external threat.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 17 Jun — Santa Cruz: Civic committee announces 3-day transport blockade to protest economic policy; supply chain disruptions across eastern Bolivia.
15 Jun — La Paz: Arce government and Morales faction exchange formal accusations in congress; political crisis deepens with no resolution mechanism.
13 Jun — Chapare: Cocalero protests over alternative development funding; roads briefly blocked.
countries.coa: Bolivia faces political instability rather than security crisis. MAS internal factional conflict creates governance uncertainty. No armed conflict at scale expected within 90 days.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: 16 Jun — Santo Domingo: Government deports 1,800 irregular migrants in biweekly operation; Haiti border closures extended.
14 Jun — Dajabón: Border trade continues restricted due to proximity of Haitian gang activity in adjacent Artibonite zone.
countries.coa: Dominican Republic faces Haiti spillover risks but maintains internal security stability. Primary monitoring requirement: Haitian gang advance toward Dominican border zone.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 17 Jun — Kingston: West Kingston state of emergency enters fourth consecutive month; crime statistics show 18% reduction vs. prior year period.
15 Jun — Montego Bay: Tourism zone security operation arrests 3 gang members; tourist facilities unaffected.
countries.coa: Jamaica faces persistent gang violence in Kingston and tourism zone security challenges. No geopolitical or state-level threat; organised crime is the primary concern.
